coredata.dc:title: A new tactics for the detection of S. aureus via paper based geno-interface incorporated with graphene nano dots and zeolites
coredata.dc:creator: Mathur, Ashish
Gupta, Rathin
Kondal, Sidharth
Wadhwa, Shikha
Pudake, Ramesh N.
Shivani,
Kansal, Ruby
Pundir, C.S.
Narang, Jagriti
coredata.dc:description: Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) is a pathogenic bacteria which causes infectious diseases and food poisoning. Current diagnostic methods for infectious disease require sophisticated instruments, long analysis time and expensive reagents which restrict their application in resource-limited settings. Electrochemical paper based analytical device (EPAD) was developed by integrating graphene nano dots (GNDs) and zeolite (Zeo) using specific DNA probe. The ssDNA/GNDs-Zeo modified paper based analytical device (PAD) was characterized using cyclic voltammetry (CV) and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV). The genosensor was optimized at pH7.4 and incubation temperature of 30°C. A linear current response with respect to target DNA concentrations was obtained. The limit of detection (LOD) of the proposed sensor was found out to be 0.1nM. The specificity was confirmed by introducing non-complimentary target DNA to ssDNA/GNDs-Zeo modified PAD. The suitability of the proposed EPAD genosensor was demonstrated with fruit juice samples mixed with S. aureus. The proposed EPAD genosensor is a low cost, highly specific, easy to fabricate diagnostic device for detection of S. aureus bacteria which requires very low sample volume and minimum analysis time of 10s.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Staphylococcus aureus
DNA
Electrochemical detection
Genosensor
Paper electrodes

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of the EPAD grafted with GNDs-Zeo nanocomposite
Application of ssDNA/GNDs-Zeo/PAD in fruit juice samples
Electrochemical characterization of GNDs-Zeo modified PAD

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology, Government of Kerala

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The present work was supported to one of the author (Dr. Ashish Mathur) by SERB, Department of Science and Technology (DST) , (Grant num SB/FTP/ETA-0025/2014 ) India. Thanks to all scientists referenced throughout the paper, whose work has guided the way to present study.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (i) Scanning electron microscope (SEM) images of i) GNDs-Zeo nanocomposite, (inset) GNDs shown as bright dots on zeolite structures; ii) Zeolite structure, (inset) nano-crystalline zeolite (iii) UV–Vis absorption spectrum of GNDs and GNDs-Zeo nanocomposite (iv) EDX spectrum of GNDs-Zeo nano- composite; Inset showing elemental composition of the formed nanocomposite (v) FTIR spectrum of Zeolite, GNDs and GNDs-Zeo Nanocomposite.

a) Cyclic voltammograms of a) bare, GNDs and GNDs-Zeo nanocomposites b) ZeO-GNDs/PAD with different probe ssDNA concentration c) ssDNA/GNDs-Zeo/PAD (50μM ssDNA) with different incubation times d) bare, GNDs-Zeo modified PAD, ssDNA/GNDs-Zeo/PAD and dsDNA/GNDs-Zeo/PAD electrodes. All experiments were performed with 1mM MB in 0.1M sodium phosphate buffer (pH7) at 100 mVs−1 in the potential range −1.0 to +1.0V.

a) Peak currents vs square root of potential (sweep rate 10–100mV/s); b) log (peak current) vs log V) cyclic voltammograms of GNDs-Zeo modified electrode at different scan rates ranging from 10 to 100mVs in the presence of 0.1M MB in sodium phosphate buffer (50mM, pH7.0).
Cyclic voltammograms of ssDNA/GNDs-Zeo/PAD a) at pH5.8–8.0 (b) in the temperature range of 15–40°C in the potential range +1.0 to −1.0V and at a scan rate of 100mV/s.

CV response of hybridization with S. aureus target DNA on DNA ssDNA/GNDs-Zeo/PAD a) in the higher concentration range (b) in the lower concentration range in 0.1M sodium phosphate buffer and 1mM MB. DPV; 10mV amplitude, for 1mM MB in 0.1M sodium phosphate buffer (pH7) at 5mVs−1analysis of hybridization c) with higher concentration ranges: Inset: log (concentration) of the target DNA in the higher concentration range and Ia (d) with higher concentration ranges: Inset: shows the log (concentration) of the target DNA in the lower concentration ranges and Ia.
Fabrication of the GNDs-Zeo modified PAD, ssDNA/GNDs-Zeo/PAD and dsDNA/GNDs-Zeo/PAD.

head.ce:title: A new tactics for the detection of S. aureus via paper based geno-interface incorporated with graphene nano dots and zeolites

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Amity Institute of Nanotechnology, Amity University, Noida 201301, UP, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Botany, Kurukshetra University, Kurukshetra 136119, Haryana, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biochemistry, MDU, Rohtak, Haryana, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) is a pathogenic bacteria which causes infectious diseases and food poisoning. Current diagnostic methods for infectious disease require sophisticated instruments, long analysis time and expensive reagents which restrict their application in resource-limited settings. Electrochemical paper based analytical device (EPAD) was developed by integrating graphene nano dots (GNDs) and zeolite (Zeo) using specific DNA probe. The ssDNA/GNDs-Zeo modified paper based analytical device (PAD) was characterized using cyclic voltammetry (CV) and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV). The genosensor was optimized at pH7.4 and incubation temperature of 30°C. A linear current response with respect to target DNA concentrations was obtained. The limit of detection (LOD) of the proposed sensor was found out to be 0.1nM. The specificity was confirmed by introducing non-complimentary target DNA to ssDNA/GNDs-Zeo modified PAD. The suitability of the proposed EPAD genosensor was demonstrated with fruit juice samples mixed with S. aureus. The proposed EPAD genosensor is a low cost, highly specific, easy to fabricate diagnostic device for detection of S. aureus bacteria which requires very low sample volume and minimum analysis time of 10s.

ce:section.ce:para: S. aureus is an anaerobic, gram negative bacterium which causes contamination of food [1]. Most of the strains of S. aureus are pathogenic which causes common mild infection including vomiting in initial stages and if not treated in early stage may lead to pneumonia (infection of lungs), bacteremia (bloodstream infection), gastrointestinal infection, osteomyelitis (bone infection), meningitis (brain infection), endocarditis (heart infection), toxic shock syndrome which may be lethal [2–6]. S. aureus is also linked with the autoimmune disease and presents a threat for causing chronic inflammatory disease lupus [7]. Current detection methods of S. aureus infection are culture test, biochemical assays [8], polymerase chain reaction (PCR) and metal electrode based sensors [9]. All conventional methods are time consuming, expensive, non-specific, and less sensitive. Geno-biosensors may possibly be the best alternative for sensitive and specific detection of the disease [10,11]. The present work is aimed to fabricate a rapid, sensitive and cost effective method for the detection of S. aureus. The method described the use of graphene nano dots (GNDs) and zeolites (Zeo) [12,13] nanocomposite immobilized on the paper based analytical device (PAD) for specific DNA detection of S. aureus.

Fabrication of facile, rapid, and cost-effective point-of-care (POC) diagnostic assays for infectious diseases predominantly in resource restricted settings remain an urgent requirement. POC diagnostics help in providing the prospects for early detection of disease which may facilitate timely management to resolve healthcare problems. Economical platforms such as PAD offer an exciting possibility in the area of POC diagnostics. PAD is light weight, flexible, cost-effective, mass-producible and disposable. Therefore, PAD is replacing the use of expensive metal based electrodes in diagnostic assays which will benefit health care professionals and patients.

In the present approach, we report an electrochemical sensing of DNA specific to S. aureus by incorporating GNDs-Zeo nanocomposites on the conductive ink decorated cellulose paper. Paper electrodes are better than screen printed electrodes in terms of cost-effectiveness, mass production and are not affected by external environment conditions while screen printed electrodes corrode easily. Intervention of nano-composites provides biocompatible environment to DNA and enhances sensing signal. Zeolites are known for their porous structural framework with large surface area, chemical stability, non-toxicity and bio-compatibility. Large surface area enhances the immobilization of DNA and imparts biocompatibility to the electrodes. However, zeolites are non-conductive. Graphene nanomaterials, on the other hand, are often considered as wonder material owing to their subatomic layer thickness exhibiting quantum effects. High electron mobility, theoretically high surface area of ~2630m2 g−1, optical transmittance (~97.7%) and good electrical conductivity are some of the unique properties of graphene reported in literature [14,15]. Graphene nanodots, therefore are considered in this work to take benefit from quantum size effects and induce conductive behavior to the electrode. We herein report the application of Electrochemical Paper based Analytical Device (EPAD) for the detection of specific DNA of S. aureus in a fruit juice sample. Consequently, we established that paper based platforms can be extensively used in multiple settings which include medical diagnostic and biology laboratories. The blend of sensing mechanisms on paper provides a potential prospect to develop diagnostic assays that meet the necessities of POC detection. The fabricated genosensor showed rapid, highly specific and linear response with low detection limit.

Sodium dihydrogen orthophosphate (monobasic) (purity 98%), di- Sodium Hydrogen orthophosphate (dibasic) (99.5%), citric acid, sodium hydroxide (99.5%), and methylene blue (were purchased from Fisher Scientific, India. Zeolite Y powder (sodium Y zeolite) was purchased from Sigma Aldrich, India. Graphene nano-dots were synthesized in-house by pyrolysis of citric acid. All the reagents used in the experiments were prepared in doubly distilled water (DDW). The 18-mer synthetic oligonucleotides were purchased from Avantor Performance Materials, India. The base sequences were as follows [16]:
Staphylococcus aureus DNA capture probe:
5′- CGC CGT TAT CTG TTT GTG -3′
Staphylococcus aureus DNA detection probe:
3′- CAC AAA CAG ATA ACG GCG -5′
Staphylococcus aureus DNA detection probe:
10mM Tris-HCl, 1mM EDTA, pH8. (TE solution) was used to make all the stock solutions of the oligonucleotides and thereafter kept at 4°C.

The optimization and analysis of the fabricated electrode and sensing of the hybridized S. aureus DNA were performed using CV and DPV on Autolab-PGSTAT-10, Eco Chemie, Utrecht, Netherlands electrochemical analysis system and Nova software package. The characterization of the synthesized nanocomposite was carried out using Zeiss EVO 18448 scanning electron microscope (SEM), Shimadzu UV 2600 UV–Vis spectrophotometer and Shimadzu 8700 FTIR spectrophotometer in the region of 4500–500cm−1 with 4cm−1 resolution. The samples for SEM were prepared by gold coating using Quorum SC7620 sputter coater prior to SEM analysis. The elemental analysis of nanocomposite was carried out by using Energy dispersive X ray (EDX) Analysis (Bruker AXS Microanalysis, GmbH Berlin, Germany) attached with SEM.

GNDs were synthesized by pyrolysis of citric acid (CA). This synthesis method minimizes the number of steps involved in the synthesis process and the need for post-treatment with surface passivating agents to increase their water solubility [17]. CA was heated upto about 250°C until it melted. The color of the liquid CA changed to pale yellow and then to orange eventually. The liquid CA was then added dropwise to NaOH (1M; 100mL) with vigorous stirring. The pH of the final solution was neutralized and subsequently lyophilized. GNDs obtained were used for the synthesis of GNDs-Zeo nanocomposite [16–19].

The nano-composite was prepared by adding different ratios of GNDs and zeolite (w/w) i.e. 4:1, 8:1 and 12:1 in adequate amount of DDW. The solution was ultra-sonicated for 1h to allow even dispersion of the composite. The composite was drop deposited onto the working surface of EPAD for electrochemical analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of the EPAD grafted with GNDs-Zeo nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: For the fabrication of EPAD, cellulose paper was used as a substrate. The electrodes were printed on paper using standard screen printing procedures employing conductive carbon ink as the electrode base. The ink was then allowed to dry at room temperature. The electrodes were printed according to the design as shown in Scheme 1 . A microfluidic well on top of the electrode acted as the electrochemical cell for the paper electrode and restricted outflow of the electrolyte to areas other than the working area. The well of the required dimensions was developed using wax which was heated at 150°C for 10min. This enabled the wax to flow through the thickness of the paper and hence created hydrophobic barriers. Similar conductivity of all the paper electrodes was ensured before employing them for electrochemical analysis. After the preparation of electrodes, dispersed solution of GNDs-Zeo nanocomposite (3μL) was then drop casted carefully on the working electrode and allowed to dry at room temperature to form an even coating of the nanocomposite.

The capture probe was optimized by using various concentrations i.e. 10, 20, 50, and 100μM concentrations for electrochemical study. Different concentrations of probe DNA were prepared by diluting the original 100μM ssDNA solution with Tris-EDTA (TE) buffer. The ssDNA capture probe (5′- CGC CGT TAT CTG TTT GTG -3′) of S. aureus (5μL; 50mM) was immobilized on GNDs- Zeo-modified EPAD and allowed to dry at 4°C for 1h. GNDs- Zeo modified EPAD electrodes were tested for their electrochemical performance using CV in the potential range −1.0 to +1.0V in sodium phosphate buffer (0.1M, pH7.4) containing 0.1mM methylene blue. The sensor working conditions such as temperature, pH and scan rate were also optimized. The fabricated electrodes were tested for various concentrations of target DNA. The optimized electrode was then tested for target DNA - (3′- CAC AAA CAG ATA ACG GCG -5′) in the concentration range of 0.1nM–100μM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of ssDNA/GNDs-Zeo/PAD in fruit juice samples
ce:section.ce:para: The prepared geno-sensor was also tested with fruit juice samples. In the present method, fruit juice samples were diluted with the 5mL of PBS, pH7.5. After this, the sample was prepared and centrifuged for 10min at 10,000rpm. Afterwards prepared juices were spiked with target DNA to detect the performance of the prepared genosensor. The known equal amount of the target DNA and DNA from other possible pathogens found in food samples (E. coli, E. faecalis, S. aureus etc.) were also added for specificity of the proposed sensor towards S. aureus. Afterwards, the CV study was carried out for detection of hybridization of DNA in spiked apple juice at the electrode surface.

Structural and morphological characterization of nanomaterial was performed by SEM, EDX, UV–Vis, and FTIR. SEM image (Fig. 1 (ii)) depicts the crystalline structure of zeolite. Upon nanocomposite formation, Graphene can be clearly seen as bright dots (~40nm) over the surface of crystalline zeolite. Fig. 1 (iii) shows the UV absorption spectra for GNDs-Zeo nanocomposite and GND particles. The UV absorption peak for GNDs spectra at 365nm confirms the formation of graphene dots. A shift in the UV absorption peak from 365nm to ~350nm may indicate composite formation [12]. The EDX spectrum of the nano-composite is shown in Fig. 1 (iv). The spectrum shows peaks for Carbon, Oxygen, Aluminium, Sodium and Silicon. FTIR spectroscopy further confirmed the nano-composite formation as shown in Fig. 4(v) [13]. The FTIR spectra of GNDs show peak broadening at 3311.84cm−1 (OH stretching), 1689.14cm−1 (CO stretching), 1565.81cm−1 (CC stretching), 1385.82cm−1 (CH graphitic skeletal ring) vibrations, and 1078.23cm−1 (COH stretching) [13,17]. The peaks for zeolite at 989.50cm−1, 790.80cm−1, 571.30cm−1, and 447.50cm−1 were observed due to zeolitic water, framework vibrations and vibrations due to AlO or SiO bonds [12,18]. Peaks similar to GNDs and zeolite were observed in the GNDs-Zeo nanocomposite, hence further confirming the nanocomposite formation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical characterization of GNDs-Zeo modified PAD
ce:section.ce:para: Cyclic voltammetry (CV) was conducted to determine the optimum ratio of GNDs and Zeolite in nano-composites, concentration of probe ssDNA S. aureus and immobilization time of the ssDNA for the fabrication of EPAD. Varying ratios of GNDs and Zeolite were used for the nano-composite optimization were 4:1, 8:1 and 12:1. The CV results (Fig. 2 (a)) indicated that GNDs modified PAD showed enhanced sensing signal as compared to nano-composites (GNDs-Zeo) with varying concentrations. In this study, zeolite was added to enhance the biocompatibility of the electrodes for DNA immobilization whereas GNDs were added to induce conductive behavior in the nanocomposite. The results confirmed that with increase in GNDs concentration there is an increase in the current response. The optimum ratio of 12:1 (GNDs: zeolite) was considered for subsequent experiments.

For immobilization of ssDNA capture probe on to the circular working region of PAD, various concentrations (i.e. 10, 20, 50 and 100μM) were taken as shown in Fig. 2 (b). It was observed that the current response gradually increased with decreasing concentrations of ssDNA. The increased current response at lower concentrations is due to better guanine – MB (methylene blue) interaction or due to ineffective immobilization of ssDNA. However, at higher concentration, the interaction of guanine bases of ssDNA with MB is inhibited. Hence, the optimum ssDNA capture probe concentration was taken to be 50μM. The ssDNA modified electrodes were then studied for immobilization time optimization. The electrodes were incubated for 20, 60 and 120min as shown in Fig. 2 (c). The optimum current response was observed for GNDs-Zeo modified electrodes incubated with ssDNA for 60min at 4°C.

As evident from Fig. 2(d), the current response for GNDs-Zeo modified electrodes is substantially increased as compared to that of bare electrode (PAD without GNDs-Zeo). When probe was immobilized on to the circular working region of PAD, the sensing signal of bare electrode was decreased as compared to nanocomposite modified PAD due to insulating nature of DNA. However ssDNA showed increased response as compared to hybridized target DNA due to the presence of MB. MB act as a hybridization indicator, in ssDNA MB showed increased response due to interaction with free guanine residues. However, in dsDNA, MB gets intercalated between guanine-cytosine complimentary base pairs. This decrease in current response from ssDNA capture probe to dsDNA detection probe confirms the hybridization of the complimentary strands [20–22].

CV of ssDNA/GNDs-Zeo modified PAD was studied for optimum performance and stability at varying scan rates. Fig. 3 shows the sensor response at potential sweep rates varying from 10 to 100mV/s. As the scan rate increased, the current response also increased. The anodic (Ia) and cathodic (Ic) peak current ratio was observed to be close to unity indicating the stability of the electrochemical response of the sensor. Fig. 3 (a) shows linear increase in peak current with the square root of scan rate. A linear increase in peak current indicates slow and reversible electron transfer kinetics which further confirms the stability in sensor performance. Fig. 3 (b) shows a plot of log (peak current) against log (scan rate). The regression coefficient was observed to be close to unity. This indicates diffusion controlled thin layer voltammetry mechanism of the electrochemical sensor.

The ssDNA/GNDs-Zeo/PAD was further tested at different pH in the range 5.8–8.0. A decreased amperometric response was observed at lower pH values as shown in Fig. 4 (a). Maximum current was obtained at pH7.4, therefore all the subsequent experiments were conducted at pH7.4. The ssDNA/GNDs-Zeo/PAD was also calibrated for temperature in the range 15 - 40°C (Fig. 4 (b)). A steady current was observed at all temperatures above 30°C, therefore 30°C was selected as an optimum temperature for further studies.

Different concentrations of complimentary target DNA in the range of 102–10−4 μM were employed for the hybridization study on the ssDNA/GNDs-Zeo modified PAD. Electrodes were prepared using 50μM ssDNA capture probe. CV and DPV studies were conducted and the peak currents were studied for various hybridization concentrations (Fig. 5 ). The insets of Fig. 5c and d show a plot between the target concentrations and relative anodic peak current (Ia) values for higher (0.5–100μM) and lower (10−4–10−1 μM) concentration ranges respectively. Ia values show linearity with Ip (A)=−8.343x+2.658 and regression coefficient R2 =0.992 for higher concentration range (Fig. 5c inset). A linear equation Ip (A)=−6.872x+2.251 with regression coefficient R2 =0.96 is obtained for lower concentration range (Fig. 5d inset). LOD (limit of detection) was calculated to be 10−4 μM or 0.1nM. It was observed that as the concentration of the target DNA decreases, the current response gradually increases. This phenomenon was confirmed by the DPV analysis which showed similar behavior. This can be attributed to the fact that the interaction between MB and guanine base pairs increases with decrease in concentration of target DNA as less number of capture DNA probes are hybridized [23]. As the concentration of target DNA is increased, MB intercalates between the guanine and cytosine base pairs and the current response decreases consequently [16].

The ssDNA/GNDs-Zeo/PAD was also tested for selectivity by introducing non-complimentary ssDNA into the working area of the electrode. Selectivity and specificity were further validated using another set of paper based electrodes. CV response was then recorded under optimized conditions as shown in Fig. S1. It was observed that the current response by the hybridized dsDNA PAD was lower in comparison to that of ssDNA modified electrode. This was attributed to the decreased number of free guanine bases for interaction with MB. The response in case of hybridized dsDNA decreases in comparison to ssDNA while the response of probe ssDNA was found to be same as that of non-complimentary ssDNA. Hence, it was concluded that the nanocomposite modified sensor was highly selective towards target DNA.

The prepared ssDNA/GNDs-Zeo/PAD was validated with fruit juice containing target DNA sample employing CV technique. The known equal amount of the target DNA and DNA from other possible pathogens found in food samples (E. coli, E. faecalis, S. aureus etc.) were added. CV was then recorded under optimized conditions as shown in Fig. S2. The peak current of the sensor with pathogens other than S. aureus were found to be almost same as immobilized ssDNA while the target DNA (S. aureus) shows lower Ia value confirming the hybridization of target DNA and hence selectivity towards S. aureus detection. This further confirms the specificity and validates the sensor fabricated here.

Paper based sensing matrices are potent substrates to make cost-effective and disposable point of care disease detection devices particularly for the resource-restricted settings. In the current work, we demonstrated the suitability of paper based matrices employing Graphene nano dots and Zeolite for the detection of S. aureus. Electrochemical paper based analytical device (EPAD) developed and optimized at pH7.4, incubation temperature of 30°C allows highly specific, selective, sensitive, and rapid detection of S. aureus with linear current response and limit of detection (LOD) ~0.1nM. Further, these platforms may potentially be tailored to detect other pathogens and bio-targets with known biomarkers.

The present work was supported to one of the author (Dr. Ashish Mathur) by SERB, Department of Science and Technology (DST), (Grant num SB/FTP/ETA-0025/2014) India. Thanks to all scientists referenced throughout the paper, whose work has guided the way to present study.
The authors claim that there is no conflict of interest and the work is original.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijbiomac.2018.01.143.
